meta.focus: Introduce the brand, establish credibility, seed the wake-up call narrative, begin waitlist capture

reels.hook: I spent £200 on fitness apps last year. Not one of them was built for me.
reels.script:
I spent £200 on fitness apps last year. Not one of them was built for me.

I'm in my 40s. I've got a demanding job, inconsistent sleep, and I genuinely don't recover the way I did at 25. Every app I tried assumed I could train five days a week, bounce back overnight, and push hard regardless of how my body was actually feeling.

So I started digging into the science. And it turns out — your body after 40 responds differently to training. Recovery windows are longer. HRV matters more. And the biggest gains don't come from training harder — they come from training smarter.

So I'm building Fitovo. An app that actually thinks the way you think. That uses your Apple Watch data — your HRV, your sleep, your resting heart rate — to tell you whether today is a push day or a recovery day.

I'm sharing the whole journey here — the research, the setbacks, the wins. Follow along if you want to be the first to know when we launch.
reels.cta: Follow so you don't miss the launch — and comment EARLY if you want on the waitlist.
reels.caption:
I spent £200 on fitness apps last year.

Not one of them was built for me.

Every app I tried assumed I was 25 — that I could train five days a week, recover overnight, and push hard no matter how I was actually feeling.

So I started researching.

Turns out, training after 40 is genuinely different. HRV, recovery windows, readiness-based scheduling — the science exists. It's just not in any app yet.

So I'm building one.

Fitovo uses your Apple Watch biometrics to tell you when to push and when to pull back — no guesswork, no guilt.

Comment EARLY below and I'll send you the waitlist link directly. 👇

reels.hook: Your VO₂max is declining right now. Every year. After 40, it doesn't stop.
reels.script:
Your VO₂max is declining right now. Every year. After 40, it doesn't stop.

VO₂max — your body's ability to use oxygen during exercise — drops roughly 10% per decade after 40 if you do nothing about it.

And here's why that should wake you up: a study published in the European Heart Journal found that VO₂max is one of the single strongest predictors of longevity. More predictive than cholesterol. More predictive than blood pressure.

But here's the good news. This is one of the most trainable metrics in your body — at any age.

The Norwegian 4x4 protocol — four minutes hard, three minutes easy, four times through — has been shown in multiple studies to increase VO₂max significantly in as little as eight weeks.

Sixteen minutes of real effort. Once or twice a week.

I'll break down exactly how to do it in the next video. Follow so you don't miss it.
reels.cta: Follow — the Norwegian 4x4 breakdown drops this week.
reels.caption:
Your VO₂max is declining right now. Every year after 40.

VO₂max — the measure of how efficiently your body uses oxygen — falls roughly 10% per decade without intervention.

A study in the European Heart Journal ranked it as one of the strongest predictors of longevity. More than cholesterol. More than blood pressure.

The good news?

It's highly trainable — even in your 50s and 60s.

The Norwegian 4x4 protocol: 4 minutes hard × 4 rounds. That's 16 minutes of real work. Studies show significant VO₂max gains in just 8 weeks.

Breaking it down in full this week. Follow so you don't miss it. 👀

reels.hook: After 35, your body starts quietly eating your muscle. Most people don't notice until it's too late.
reels.script:
After 35, your body starts quietly eating your muscle. Most people don't notice until it's too late.

Sarcopenia — that's the medical term for age-related muscle loss — takes 3 to 5 percent of your muscle mass every decade. By your 60s, you could have lost 20% of your peak muscle. By your 70s, even more.

And here's what most people miss: this isn't just about how you look or how strong you feel at the gym. Muscle is your metabolic currency. It regulates your blood sugar. It protects your joints. It literally keeps you walking, climbing stairs, and picking up your grandchildren.

Falls are the leading cause of injury-related death in over-65s. Almost all of them trace back to muscle weakness and poor balance — both entirely trainable.

The fix is not bodybuilding. Two to three resistance sessions a week, progressively loaded, is all the evidence points to.

Save this — I'll be sharing exactly what that looks like for your 40s and 50s.
reels.cta: Save this — and comment STRONG if you want the full resistance training guide sent to you.

reels.hook: Norwegian researchers found the most effective cardio protocol ever studied. It takes 16 minutes.
reels.script:
Norwegian researchers found the most effective cardio protocol ever studied. It takes 16 minutes.

The Norwegian 4x4 was developed at the Norwegian University of Science and Technology. Here's how it works.

Warm up for 10 minutes at easy pace. Then: four minutes at 85 to 95 percent of your max heart rate. Three minutes easy. Repeat four times. Cool down.

Total hard work: 16 minutes.

In a landmark study, people who followed this protocol for 8 weeks saw VO₂max improvements that outperformed every other cardio protocol studied — including longer steady-state sessions.

For adults over 40, this is significant. You don't have time for 90-minute sessions. You do have 16 minutes.

You can run, cycle, row, or use any cardio machine. Your Apple Watch will tell you when you're in the right heart rate zone.

Fitovo will guide you through these sessions automatically — and only schedule them on days your biometrics say you're ready.

Comment 4X4 below and I'll send you the full protocol breakdown.
reels.cta: Comment 4X4 and I'll send you the full protocol.
reels.caption:
Norwegian researchers found the most effective cardio protocol ever studied.

It takes 16 minutes of real effort.

The Norwegian 4x4:

🔵 10 min warm-up
🔴 4 min at 85–95% max HR
🔵 3 min easy recovery
× 4 rounds

Studies at the Norwegian University of Science and Technology showed greater VO₂max improvements than ANY other protocol — including longer steady-state sessions.

You can run, cycle, row. Your Apple Watch tells you when you're in zone.

Fitovo will schedule these sessions on days your body is actually ready for them.

reels.hook: Three exercises. No equipment. Your knees, hips, and back at 70 will thank you.
reels.script:
Three exercises. No equipment. Your knees, hips, and back at 70 will thank you.

Number one: the split squat. One foot forward, one foot back. Lower your back knee toward the floor — slowly. This builds the quad and glute strength that keeps your knees tracking correctly for decades. Three sets of eight per side.

Number two: the hip hinge. Stand hip-width apart, soft bend in the knees, push your hips back — not down. Feel your hamstrings load. This is the pattern that protects your lower back and builds posterior chain strength without a single weight. Three sets of ten.

Number three: the dead hang. Find a bar, a beam, even a sturdy door frame. Hang for 30 seconds. This decompresses your spine, builds grip strength — which is strongly linked to longevity — and improves shoulder health over time. Daily if you can.

These aren't trending. They're not flashy. But the research on what keeps people functional into their 70s and 80s keeps pointing at exactly these patterns.

Save this and come back to it.
reels.cta: Save this — and follow for weekly exercises backed by actual science.
reels.caption:
Three exercises. No equipment. No gym.

Do these consistently and your body will thank you for decades.

1️⃣ Split squat — quad and glute strength, knee tracking
3 × 8 per side

2️⃣ Hip hinge — posterior chain, lower back protection
3 × 10

3️⃣ Dead hang — spine decompression, grip strength, shoulder health
30 seconds, daily

Grip strength alone has been shown in multiple studies to predict longevity independently. The dead hang is free medicine.

Save this 🔖 — and follow for weekly evidence-backed tips for training in your 40s, 50s, and beyond.

carousels.title: 5 things that happen to your body after 40 (that nobody tells you)
slides:
5 things that happen to your body after 40
(that nobody tells you)

1. You lose muscle faster than you think

3–5% per decade after 30.
By 70, you could have lost 40% of your peak mass.

2. Your VO₂max declines every year

~10% per decade without training.
Lower VO₂max = shorter healthspan.

3. Recovery takes longer

Your muscles need 48–72hrs between hard sessions.
Pushing through it is working against you.

4. HRV becomes your most important metric

Heart rate variability tells you more about your readiness than any fitness score.

5. Falls become your #1 injury risk

Strength training is the #1 prevention.
This is why muscle loss isn't just about fitness.

The good news?

All of this is trainable.
Your 40s and 50s are the investment window.

Fitovo is built around these facts.

Join the waitlist → link in bio.
Comment EARLY and we'll DM you the link.
carousels.caption:
Nobody handed you this information at 40.

So here it is.

Swipe through — share with someone who needs to see it. 👉

carousels.title: How to read your Apple Watch HRV (and why it changes everything after 40)
slides:
How to read your Apple Watch HRV
(and why it changes everything after 40)

HRV = Heart Rate Variability

The variation in time between heartbeats.
Higher HRV = more recovered and resilient.

Where to find it

Health app → Browse → Heart → Heart Rate Variability
Your Watch measures it during sleep automatically.

Don't compare your number to others

HRV is highly individual.
Track your own baseline — that's the only number that matters.

Low HRV day?

Your body is under stress.
Go easy: a walk, light movement, or full rest.
Training hard will set you back.

High HRV day?

You're recovered.
This is your green light for a hard session.
Use it.

Most fitness apps ignore this completely.

Fitovo is built around it.
Every session is scheduled around your actual readiness.

Follow for more evidence-backed training tips.

Waitlist → link in bio.
carousels.caption:
Your Apple Watch has been quietly measuring one of the most useful metrics in exercise science.

HRV tells you more about your readiness to train than any fitness score or streak.

Here's how to actually use it. 👉

Save this — you'll want to come back to it.
